intro: An area chart from CSV data is one of the clearest ways to show how a value builds or changes over time. If you have a spreadsheet with dates and numeric values, you can turn it into an area chart in Excel or Google Sheets with a few clicks. Or you can upload the CSV to Distill and get the chart generated for you in seconds, no setup required.

whatIs.heading: What Is an Area Chart?
whatIs.body: An area chart is a line chart with the space between the line and the axis filled in. The filled area makes it easier to see volume and cumulative change at a glance. It works best with continuous data over time, such as weekly sales totals, monthly website traffic, or daily temperature readings.

whenToUse.heading: When Should You Use an Area Chart?
bullets: You want to show how a single metric accumulates or changes over a time period, such as monthly revenue or running totals.
You are comparing two or three series and want to show their relative contribution to a whole, using a stacked area chart.
Your data has a clear time axis and you want to emphasize the magnitude of change, not just the direction of the trend.

excelSteps.heading: How to Make an Area Chart in Excel
steps: Open your CSV in Excel by going to File > Open, selecting your file, and confirming the delimiter settings in the import wizard.
Arrange your data so the date or time column is in column A and your numeric values are in column B (or further right for multiple series).
Select the data range you want to chart, including the header row.
Go to the Insert tab, click the Charts group, and select the Area chart icon. Choose '2-D Area' for a standard area chart or 'Stacked Area' to compare multiple series.
Click on the chart title to rename it, and use the Chart Design tab to adjust colors and axis labels as needed.
To save the chart, right-click it and choose 'Save as Picture', or keep it embedded in the workbook and export the sheet.

sheetsSteps.heading: How to Make an Area Chart in Google Sheets
steps: Upload your CSV to Google Drive, then open it in Google Sheets by right-clicking and selecting Open with > Google Sheets.
Confirm that dates are in the first column and numeric values are in the columns to the right.
Select the data range you want to visualize, including headers.
Click Insert > Chart. Google Sheets will suggest a chart type automatically.
In the Chart Editor panel on the right, open the Chart type dropdown and select 'Area chart'. For multiple series, choose 'Stacked area chart'.
Customize the title, axis labels, and colors using the Customize tab in the Chart Editor, then close the panel.

distillSection.heading: A Faster Way: Skip the Manual Steps with Distill
distillSection.body: If you just want to see your data as a chart without configuring axes or adjusting chart type menus, Distill is a quicker path. Upload your CSV and Distill reads every column, picks appropriate chart types, and renders them instantly. For time-based data, it generates area and line charts automatically and writes a short plain-language narrative summarizing what the data shows. The result is a shareable dashboard you can send to anyone with a link. No account is needed and the free tier supports CSV files up to 1,000 rows.

gifAlt: Screen recording of a CSV file being dragged into Distill, which automatically generates an area chart showing values over time alongside a written summary.

faqs.q: Can I make an area chart without Excel?
faqs.a: Yes. Google Sheets supports area charts natively and is free. You can also upload your CSV directly to Distill, which generates the chart for you without any manual configuration.

faqs.q: What is the difference between a line chart and an area chart?
faqs.a: An area chart is essentially a line chart with the region below the line filled with color. The fill helps readers perceive volume and cumulative change more easily than a bare line. For simple trend comparisons, a line chart is often cleaner; when magnitude matters, an area chart adds useful visual weight.
Yes. Distill's free tier supports CSV files up to 1,000 rows and includes all chart types. No account is required to try it.
